Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty chaotic most of the time. I'd try to set aside time on weekends when I wasn't working, but with seven online courses and limited resources, it was hard to stick to anything consistent. I'm a visual learner, so I'd spend a lot of time making diagrams and charts from the material, which took longer than just reading. Some weeks I'd manage maybe 16 hours of actual studying, but it was scattered across late nights and early mornings whenever I could grab time.
Q2: My pattern varied a lot depending on what was due and what was happening in my life. I work to support myself, so my schedule isn't really my own most of the time. When deadlines were close, I'd cram everything in, but during quieter weeks I might barely touch the coursework. It wasn't ideal, but I had to be flexible to survive.
Q3: Usually it was either a deadline looming or seeing a notification that something new was posted. Sometimes I'd log in just to check if I'd missed anything important since my attendance was pretty spotty. Fear of falling behind was a big motivator for me—I'd get this scared feeling that I was missing something crucial, and that would push me to check the LMS.
Q4: There were a few weeks where I got into a rhythm of checking in every couple of days. It happened when I found some discussion threads that were actually helpful and started interacting with other students. Having those peer connections made me want to come back. But then life would get overwhelming again, and the routine would fall apart.
Q5: The video content was really valuable to me because I learn best by seeing things explained visually. I watched a lot of the lecture videos—probably around 170 minutes worth—and I'd pause and take notes or sketch out concepts. The discussion boards were also helpful because I could see how other students understood things, and that gave me different perspectives on the material.
Q6: I ignored a lot of the reading materials and some of the optional practice exercises. With low resources, I couldn't always print things out or access them easily offline, so if something required a lot of reading on screen, I'd skip it. Some resources just felt like busywork that wouldn't directly help my grade, so I had to prioritize ruthlessly.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see a direct connection to the exams or assignments. If something was going to help me pass, I'd make time for it even when I was exhausted. I also valued anything that helped me actually understand the concepts visually rather than just memorizing facts.
Q8: Optional activities felt like a luxury I couldn't afford most of the time. Required ones had deadlines and consequences, so they got done—or at least attempted. With optional stuff, I'd think 'that would be nice to do' but then something more urgent would come up. There was always this guilt about not doing them, but I had to be realistic about my time and energy.
Q9: Absolutely. I'd download videos and PDFs when I had internet access and then study them offline later. I made a lot of handwritten notes and visual diagrams that I'd review on the bus or during breaks at work. I also used some free YouTube videos to supplement topics I didn't fully understand from the course materials.
Q10: A lot of my actual learning happened with my notebooks spread out at the kitchen table, going over diagrams I'd drawn. I'd spend hours working through problems by hand that never showed up in the LMS. Sometimes I'd watch a lecture video once online, then replay it in my head while sketching out the concepts later—that deeper processing wouldn't show up anywhere in the system.
Q11: Yeah, that was pretty much my standard approach. I'd access something once to download it or take detailed notes, then work with those materials offline for days. My internet isn't always reliable, so I learned to grab what I needed and then study independently. The LMS would show one quick access, but I might have spent five hours with that material offline.
Q12: There were definitely low points, especially mid-semester when everything seemed to pile up at once. I was scared I wouldn't make it through—that fear was constant. When I was exhausted from work and stressed about money, it became really hard to focus on coursework. The mindfulness exercises they offered helped a bit, but sometimes the overwhelm was just too much.
Q13: All of those things affected me constantly. Fatigue was huge—I'd log into the LMS intending to watch a lecture but find myself just staring at the screen. Confusion about assignment requirements made me avoid starting them until the last minute. Work responsibilities meant I'd sometimes go days without touching the course, then have to catch up frantically.
Q14: More times than I'd like to admit. I'd open materials and scroll through them without really absorbing anything because my mind was elsewhere—worrying about bills or just too tired to concentrate. Sometimes I'd have the videos playing while doing other things, which counted as 'watching' but wasn't really learning.
Q15: I postponed assignments constantly, even when I knew they were important. Part of it was feeling scared that I'd fail anyway, so why start? Part of it was just not having the mental energy after everything else in my life. It's not that I didn't care—I cared a lot—but sometimes the fear of doing something badly made me avoid it until I absolutely had to face it.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that got me to submit work. I'd have these bursts of activity right before something was due, cramming everything in at the last minute. It wasn't healthy, but without that pressure, I'd keep putting things off. The LMS probably showed these spikes of activity clustered around due dates.
Q17: When I got feedback that was encouraging or showed me I was on the right track, it motivated me to keep going. But if feedback was delayed or harsh, it made me want to avoid that course for a while. I needed that validation to feel like my effort was worth something, especially when I was struggling with low motivation.
Q18: The course structure was challenging because with seven courses, I couldn't give each one the attention it deserved. Having clear modules helped me know what to prioritize, but sometimes the structure felt rigid when I needed more flexibility. The discussion requirements actually helped keep me connected when I might have otherwise isolated myself.
Q19: The video content being available to download made engagement much easier for me. What made it harder was when assignments had unclear instructions or when there were too many small tasks to track across multiple courses. Also, some of the interactive elements required consistent internet, which I don't always have.
Q20: I think that could be someone like me who's trying hard but struggling to actually complete things. You might log in a lot, watch videos, participate in discussions, but then hit a wall when it comes to assignments because of time constraints, confusion, or just running out of energy. High activity doesn't mean everything is going smoothly.
Q21: That resonates with me somewhat. I did okay on my exam despite low attendance because I was studying offline a lot. If you just looked at my login frequency or attendance, you'd think I was barely trying. But I was putting in hours with downloaded materials and handwritten notes that the system couldn't see.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns—when you're active, what you click on, how long you spend on pages. But they miss the quality of engagement and all the offline work. They can't tell if you're actually understanding something or just going through the motions. They definitely can't capture the fear, the struggle, or the determination behind the numbers.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about learning the material and putting in effort to understand it, even when it's hard. It's not just showing up or clicking through—it's wrestling with concepts until they make sense. For me, engagement also means staying connected to other students and not giving up when things get overwhelming.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how scared I was most of the time, or how hard I had to fight to keep going with limited resources and energy. It wouldn't capture the hours I spent with handwritten notes or the mindfulness breathing I did before logging in to calm my anxiety. It can't show that passing this course means everything to me because I'm trying to climb out of a difficult situation.
